nativeRange.habitat: Shallow reef crests, lagoon patch reefs, and fore-reef slopes with moderate to strong surge and good light penetration

decorPreferences: secure attachment with gel glue or epoxy to stable rockwork
open water column access for flow and feeding
spacing from neighboring corals to prevent allelopathy

diet.notes: The Purple Tree Gorgonian is primarily photosynthetic, drawing nutrition from symbiotic zooxanthellae in its tissues. Supplemental feeding is strongly recommended two to three times per week, and the colony tends to accept phytoplankton, microplankton, Marine Snow, PhytoPlan, and finely minced mysis or frozen baby brine shrimp. Polyps tend to extend more readily at night, making evening target feeding sessions generally more effective.

goodWith: other non-stinging gorgonians with adequate spacing
avoidWith: aggressive stinging LPS corals placed in direct contact
highly turbulent laminar flow (can strip tissue)

summary: The Purple Tree Gorgonian (Muriceopsis flavida) is a photosynthetic Caribbean sea fan growing into branching, tree-shaped purple colonies up to 75 cm tall. It is generally considered intermediate to keep, requiring strong turbulent flow, moderate to moderately-high lighting, and regular supplemental feeding.
careNotes:
The Purple Tree Gorgonian (Muriceopsis flavida) belongs to the family Plexauridae and is zooxanthellate — meaning it harbors symbiotic algae (zooxanthellae) in its tissues that contribute to its energy needs through photosynthesis. This distinguishes it from non-photosynthetic gorgonians such as Dendronephthya, which depend entirely on target feeding, and makes it considerably more forgiving to keep. It is generally considered one of the more beginner-accessible gorgonians, though it still requires a mature, well-established reef system.

Key care considerations:
• Flow: High, turbulent (non-laminar) water movement is essential. Gorgonians are prone to cyanobacteria and nuisance algae growth when flow is insufficient — chaotic, multi-directional flow prevents detritus from settling on branches. The branching colony structure creates many surfaces where stagnant pockets can develop, so dead spots should be actively avoided.
• Lighting: Moderate to moderately-high intensity (approximately 100–200 PAR) is generally advised. As a shallow-water Caribbean species found on reef crests between 3 and 15 m, it is adapted to good light but does not require the extreme intensities demanded by SPS corals. Mid-tank placement is typically recommended.
• Feeding: Although photosynthesis through zooxanthellae provides a significant portion of nutrition, regular supplemental feeding is strongly advised. Phytoplankton, microplankton, Marine Snow, PhytoPlan, and finely prepared frozen foods offered two to three times per week are commonly recommended. Polyp extension tends to be greater at night, so evening feeding sessions are often more productive.
• Attachment: The colony must be firmly secured to stable rockwork using gel superglue or two-part reef epoxy. A loose or tumbling colony is highly susceptible to tissue damage and algae fouling. New frags or recently acquired specimens should be given time to acclimate to flow conditions before being fully attached.
• Algae vigilance: Overgrowth of cyanobacteria or hair algae on the skeleton is a primary cause of colony decline. Adequate flow and well-maintained water quality — with nitrate and phosphate kept within 1–20 ppm and 0.01–0.1 ppm respectively — help prevent this.
• Water chemistry: Supplementation with calcium, strontium, and trace elements (iodine in particular) is commonly reported to benefit gorgonians. Alkalinity should be maintained at 8–12 dKH and calcium at 380–450 ppm.
• Colony size: Wild colonies can reach 50–75 cm in height. Captive colonies typically grow more slowly but can become substantial over years.
• Mature tank: A fully cycled, established reef system is strongly recommended. Newly set-up tanks lack the microplankton diversity that supplements gorgonian nutrition.
breedingNotes: Fragging is possible by cutting branches with bone cutters or scissors and reattaching segments to rubble or frag plugs with gel glue. Frags should be placed in moderate flow and light while they encrust and establish. In the wild, sexual reproduction occurs via broadcast spawning, but captive sexual reproduction has not been documented in the hobby.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – Purple Tree Gorgonian
primary.notes: Primary product listing. Listed scientific name as Eunicea sp.; corrected to Muriceopsis flavida per Plexauridae taxonomy and cross-source confirmation.

additional.name: AquariumDomain – Purple Brush Gorgonian Species Profile
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (temp 72–78°F, SG 1.023–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12), lighting (medium-high), flow (moderate-to-strong), feeding (2–3x/week), care level (moderate), and family Plexauridae. Agreed with primary on all high-stakes fields.

additional.name: TankFacts – Purple Brush Gorgonian
additional.notes: Cross-checked calcium (380–450 ppm), magnesium (1200–1400 ppm), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.023–1.026), kH (8–12). Confirmed zooxanthellate classification and peaceful-to-semi-aggressive behavior. Minor salinity upper bound difference (1.026 vs 1.025) — adopted 1.025 as conservative standard reef salinity.

additional.name: Reeflex – Muriceopsis flavida (Rough Sea Plume)
additional.notes: Source for depth range (3–33 m), temperature (24–27°C), adult colony height (50–75 cm), native range (Caribbean, Belize, Florida, Bahamas, Gulf of Mexico), and taxonomy (Family Plexauridae, Order Alcyonacea). NOTE: This source categorized M. flavida as azooxanthellate — this is contradicted by all other sources and standard Plexauridae literature; the photosynthetic classification has been adopted. Temperature cross-checked: 24–27°C (reeflex) vs 22–25.6°C (LiveAquaria 72–78°F) — averaged to 23–27°C.

additional.name: Seahorse Savvy – Gorgonian Corals
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters for Caribbean photosynthetic gorgonians: temp 70–74°F noted as lower bound suggestion, SG 1.020–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, calcium 350–450 ppm, Mg 1250–1350 ppm. Confirmed moderate-to-slightly-higher flow. Lower temperature and salinity bounds appear conservative; averaged across sources.
